--

Three years after starting The Odin Project, I reflect on learning backend development, building APIs, and becoming a full-stack developer.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

It’s exactly three years since I began taking The Odin Project’s full-stack JavaScript web development curriculum. When I started, I had little to no knowledge about programming, but after consistent efforts, here we are.
In case you missed the starting part of the journey, I have written about it:
· Part 1 — From Beginner to Builder: My Journey Learning JavaScript with The Odin Project
· Part 2 — From JavaScript to React: Building Real Projects with The Odin Project
As of this writing, I’ve just finished all of Odin Project’s Lessons in the Database and Node.js course, and it’s right before working on the three final full-stack projects.
I’m writing to share my experience on this journey with anyone curious about what full-stack development looks like, especially if you’re already involved in web development.
In that light, I’ve been consistently writing on this blog, and it’s only just that I share something personal about what I’ve been into since getting into React with The Odin Project.

Why I Decided to Learn Backend Development
I started learning programming because I wanted to write software for software engineers. Much of the most complex technical writing in software development centers on API and developer documentation.
As simple as it sounds, you can document APIs and Software best when you’re well conversant with industry terms and are actually using APIs. I wanted to satisfy my curiosity about how backend applications and APIs work by building some.
After finishing the React course, most of the applications I built could only run on the client (browser) and were not persistent unless I used local storage. Whenever a user opened the application on another device, they’d never get the data they saved on their first one. I needed to add a stable layer that worked for my app, and taking the backend course was the best step.
With React under my belt, I only knew one half of web development. With Node.js in the toolbox, I now had a foundation that set me up to get into the other half of web development — the backend. As I write this, I can now call myself a full-stack developer. Another reason was that by using Node.js I’d have another chance to use JavaScript and, in turn, become a stronger developer by using one language end-to-end.
The Odin Project’s NodeJS Course Was Different Than I Expected
Unlike the past courses, I’ve progressed relatively quickly in this Node.js course, and it has also been the most challenging part of this curriculum, yet the most balanced in terms of tutorials and projects. After every few lessons, a project to tie everything up by solving a problem.
Backend development at Odin begins with a strong introduction to SQL Databases. During this database course, I learned to create SQL databases and define primary keys and foreign keys; to create schemas; and to use various select statements, clauses, and functions, as well as the difference between WHERE and HAVING. After learning these, I spent some time reading the docs on SQL databases, working with complex queries, and exploring complex relationships.
Actual backend development took place next. I’d get introduced to Node.js, how to debug it, and then build a basic information site that serves different HTML files. I later learned about environment variables, which help in securing application secrets. Next in line was an intro to Express.
On learning basic Express request-response patterns, I’d then refactor my basic information to use Express instead of Node. Express helps reduce much of the boilerplate code I’d otherwise have to write by hand using Node.js. The next learning items would be routes, controllers, and views to complete the MVC pattern. After finishing these topics, the next project was a mini-message board built using Express MVC and EJS. This would be my first fully CRUD server-side rendered (SSR) application.
At this time, I’m still using mock functions because I haven’t connected any application to a real database yet. I learned how to deploy a backend application using Render. The next lessons covered forms and data handling, installing PostgreSQL, and using it with my backend. I ended up building an inventory application that uses PostgreSQL and deployed it.
What followed were authentication basics using PassportJS and building a members-only application that would allow users to see messages posted with hidden names for comments if they’re not authenticated members. Once you are authenticated and have joined the club, you can also view more stuff like who the members are and even comment on it, and so much more, like seeing the user dashboard admin.
The next item was to use database modules like pg and an object-relational mapper to simplify database work. I got into the PRISM ORM tool by reading its documentation and worked on a File Uploader Project. Before working on an explicit one, I read more about API development patterns, particularly RESTful ones, and about securing my APIs with JSON Web Tokens (JWTs).
The last lessons focused on testing my application routes and controllers, as well as database operations. It took some time to get the flow of writing unit tests, integration tests, and overall end-to-end tests. The toughest part was getting the actual perspective of the differences between them. There aren’t any more lessons in terms of the course; only three final projects are left. But new packages have been introduced and are intended for use in different projects.
This doesn’t mean I know everything or that learning stops here. On the contrary, I think I’ve only scratched the surface of programming, but I understand application end-to-end development, and I’m able to build some of my own. The best part is that by understanding applications by building, I have built a good foundation in web development.
Building Real Full-Stack Projects Changed Everything
Looking at the skill progression, for each application I’ve built in the course, I’ve had a chance to learn new skills across the web development process. The mini message board was the first to teach me about validation, how to organize a CRUD application, and to get a feel for the flow of data from a user to the database through a backend. It was also the first backend project that I deployed.
Inventory Application
Fast forward to the inventory application, we are talking about a CRUD app with a few more items to CRUD and a different data representation. Notice how I first encountered the Multer package for file handling and how to add seed data into an application’s live database, which, of course, I had to scrape from a live game-data API and clean up into usable formats for my application. The most challenging part was organizing the functionality for filtering my games in my views; consuming it on the backend and filtering at the database level were relatively easy.
Repository: https://github.com/john-walter-munene/Inventory-Application
Members only
Members Only was the first application to test authentication and authorization. While doing CRUD operations for messages is a quick task, reasoning about the functionality I wanted to expose based on whether the user is a guest, an authenticated user, or a clubhouse member was an important skill I would carry forward into future applications.
Repository: https://github.com/john-walter-munene/Members-Only
File Uploader
A step further into the file uploader application, I was now flexing the use of Object-Relational mappers and handling even more complicated table relationships. I really appreciate this project as I was building a stripped-down version of Google Drive where one could upload and CRUD files, then share expiring links.
Instead of storing images by having them navigate complex folder hierarchies using recursion, I opted to store files and folders as data in tables, linking only to parent-child relationships as part of the table content! I’d later navigate the relationship traversal using recursion and note that while the browser is limited to 1000 call stacks, the server can go further, as I’m now using native resources. Read my guide on recursive programming here.
Sharing files would use randomly generated tokens referenced for access, with an expiration time attribute that the query would use to invalidate the token in the database. Interesting, right!? And now, think about integrating file storage with Supabase. As projects grew in complexity and requirements, so did the amount of code I wrote.
Repository: https://github.com/john-walter-munene/File-Uploader
Blog API
The blog API is a backend application I’m developing to be consumed by two different frontend applications. Having gained some fluency in backend development and added two authentication strategies (Passport Local Strategy and JSON Web Tokens), I had a lot of fun designing a larger API. This is primarily a backend application, but as we chat about it, I’m actually writing two clients for it: one to view and comment on blogs, and the other as a dedicated administrator or editor client for the same API.
Repository: https://github.com/john-walter-munene/Blog-API
Full-Stack Development Isn’t Just Frontend Plus Backend
Building a full-stack web project is not only interesting but also challenging. Depending on the type of application you’re building, you’ll need to consider different approaches, such as a client-API architecture or even server-side rendered web apps. Each will have its own benefits, trade-offs, and even development patterns.
One thing I appreciate is that there are many ways to get your application running, starting either from the Database, the backend API, or even the frontend. It is important to note that, regardless of the method you choose, you’ll need to explicitly consider API architecture, authentication, authorization, database design, deployment, debugging, and security.
Instead of thinking of each technology as an isolated unit, you’ll need to get each piece working on specific tasks and all of them working in tandem. I’ve found working with full-stack SSR applications easy to build and iterate on. However, I’ve found a cleaner architecture with a client-API approach because the frontend and backend can evolve independently as long as we maintain standardized API contracts.
When working with SSR, all I have to do is serve my template and pass the right data through local variables. I do not have to think about the state, but note how my views are in tight contact with the controllers.
Where you might think of straightforward ways to minimize backend code by using fewer tables, you face the interesting challenge of creating explicit relationships and reasoning about them (think about the file uploader project).
Now, while I have an easy way to build with templates, I’m facing the problem of standardizing my styling approach, which means handling many CSS files. In cases where React gives me different ways to style my apps using CSS Modules, vanilla CSS, CSS-in-JS, and/or frameworks like Tailwind, SSR apps lock me into vanilla CSS.
While template-based applications rely on the server to handle identity, using React-based patterns means I have to work with local storage to persist an authenticated user. In places where React gives a better way to define intuitive interfaces, I have to write a dedicated API contract rather than having every useEffect track its own requests for uniformity and better testing.
Not that any approach is better than the others, but the right balance needs to be struck and the right trade-offs made. I’ll pen an article on the differences between building applications using server-side rendering and client-side API architectures in the future.
Technical Interviews Have Started Coming My Way
